severityScales.name: Expanded Disability Status Scale (EDSS)
severityScales.description: Standardized neurologic disability scale from 0 (normal) to 10 (death due to MS), adapted for NMOSD

severityScales.name: Visual acuity assessment
severityScales.description: Snellen chart, low-contrast letter acuity, automated perimetry for quantitative visual outcome

severityScales.name: Hauser Ambulation Index
severityScales.description: Assesses gait impairment from 0 (asymptomatic) to 9 (restricted to wheelchair, unable to transfer)

attackSeverity.definition: Minor sensory symptoms or visual acuity >20/40, full recovery expected with steroids
Visual acuity 20/40 to 20/200, moderate motor deficit, bowel/bladder involvement
Visual acuity <20/200, paraplegia, quadriplegia, respiratory failure requiring ventilation, or coma

historyFeatures.typicalPresentation: Acute or subacute onset of unilateral or bilateral optic neuritis and/or longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis
coreClinicalSyndromes: Optic neuritis: acute to subacute vision loss, periocular pain with eye movement, dyschromatopsia (color desaturation)
Acute transverse myelitis: bilateral limb weakness, sensory level, bladder and bowel dysfunction, Lhermitte sign
Area postrema syndrome: intractable hiccups, nausea and vomiting (most characteristic early syndrome)
historyFeatures.opticNeuritisFeatures: More severe than MS-associated optic neuritis; bilateral simultaneous involvement common; poor recovery without treatment; pain on eye movement frequent
historyFeatures.transverseMyelitisFeatures: Longitudinally extensive lesion extending 3 or more vertebral segments; flaccid paralysis acutely then spastic; severe sensory ataxia; autonomic dysfunction
brainstemSyndromes: Area postrema syndrome: intractable hiccups, nausea, vomiting - most specific for NMOSD
Diencephalic syndrome: hypersomnia, hyponatremia, hypothermia, endocrine dysfunction
Oculomotor dysfunction: nystagmus, diplopia, internuclear ophthalmoplegia
historyFeatures.relapsingCourse: More than 80% of patients have a relapsing course; untreated, 50% have permanent disability within 5 years

opticNerveExam: Reduced visual acuity from mild impairment to no light perception
Afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) in unilateral or asymmetric involvement
Color desaturation (dyschromatopsia) out of proportion to visual acuity loss
Visual field defects: central scotoma, altitudinal defects, generalized constriction
Optic disc swelling in acute optic neuritis (more common in NMOSD than MS)
Optic atrophy in chronic or recurrent cases
motorExam: Spinal cord involvement: symmetric or asymmetric weakness of limbs
Upper motor neuron signs: spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski sign
Acute flaccid paralysis during severe myelitis (spinal shock)
Cervical myelitis may affect phrenic nerve causing diaphragmatic weakness and respiratory compromise
sensoryExam: Sensory level on trunk corresponding to spinal cord lesion
Loss of pain and temperature sensation
Impaired vibration and proprioception in posterior column involvement
Lhermitte sign: electric shock sensation down spine on neck flexion
cerebellarExam: Sensory ataxia due to proprioceptive loss from dorsal column involvement
Gait impairment out of proportion to weakness

riskFactors: Female sex: female to male ratio 9:1 in AQP4-IgG positive NMOSD
Non-Caucasian ethnicity: higher prevalence in Asian, African, and Latin American populations
HLA associations: HLA-DRB1*0301, HLA-DQB1*0402, and other HLA alleles in different ethnic groups
Concurrent autoimmune diseases: systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjogren syndrome, myasthenia gravis, autoimmune thyroiditis
Infectious triggers: preceding viral or bacterial infections may trigger attacks
Pregnancy: increased attack risk in first 3-6 months postpartum
Low vitamin D levels: associated with increased attack risk
Age of onset: typically 35-45 years, later than MS; can occur in children and elderly
Family history: familial cases rare but reported; genetic predisposition suggested

subtypes.name: AQP4-IgG seropositive NMOSD
subtypes.description: Autoantibodies against aquaporin-4 water channels, accounts for 70-80% of NMOSD
subtypes.details: More severe disease course; higher relapse risk; frequently associated with other autoantibodies and autoimmune diseases; pathologically distinct from MS; more common in females

subtypes.name: MOG-IgG associated disorder (MOGAD)
subtypes.description: Autoantibodies against myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein, distinct from AQP4-NMOSD
subtypes.details: More common in children; frequently presents with ADEM-like phenotype; bilateral optic neuritis common; better recovery than AQP4-positive; spinal lesions may be shorter; less severe course overall

subtypes.name: Double seronegative NMOSD
subtypes.description: Negative for both AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG but meets clinical criteria for NMOSD
subtypes.details: Diagnosis of exclusion; requires strict adherence to diagnostic criteria; may represent other autoimmune or inflammatory CNS disorders; treatment approach similar but outcomes variable

subtypes.name: NMOSD with autoimmune comorbidity
subtypes.description: NMOSD associated with systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjogren syndrome, or other autoimmune conditions
subtypes.details: May represent two concurrent autoimmune processes or NMOSD mimicking these conditions; autoimmune serologies should be checked but attributed to NMOSD if core clinical features present

mimics: Multiple sclerosis: shorter spinal cord lesions, periventricular plaques, oligoclonal bands in CSF, less severe optic neuritis
Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM): polysymptomatic, encephalopathy, typically monophasic, more common in children
Spinal cord compression: compressive lesion on MRI, no brain findings, rapid steroid response absent
Spinal dural arteriovenous fistula: progressive myelopathy, flow voids on MRI, no relapses
Vitamin B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration): symmetric myelopathy, macrocytic anemia, low B12 levels
Neurolupus or neuro-Sjogren: systemic autoimmune features, specific autoantibodies, different CSF profile
Sarcoidosis: leptomeningeal enhancement, hilar lymphadenopathy, non-caseating granulomas
Primary CNS vasculitis: vessel wall enhancement, ischemic lesions, angiography abnormalities
Paraneoplastic myelopathy: progressive symmetric myelopathy, onconeural antibodies, underlying malignancy
HTLV-1 associated myelopathy: endemic exposure, progressive chronic myelopathy, anti-HTLV-1 antibodies

bedside: Visual acuity testing with Snellen chart and near card
Pupillary examination for afferent pupillary defect
Color vision testing with Ishihara plates
Bladder ultrasound for post-void residual volume
Spirometry: forced vital capacity to assess respiratory muscle strength in cervical myelitis
laboratory: Serum AQP4-IgG (aquaporin-4 antibody): gold standard, cell-based assay preferred for highest sensitivity and specificity
Serum MOG-IgG: myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody, cell-based assay
CSF analysis: mild pleocytosis (often >50 cells/uL) with neutrophil predominance in acute attacks; protein elevated; oligoclonal bands less frequent than MS (15-30%)
CSF AQP4-IgG: may be positive even if serum negative in some cases
Autoimmune serologies: ANA, anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB, anticardiolipin, lupus anticoagulant, anti-thyroid antibodies
Vitamin B12, folate, copper, zinc levels: rule out deficiency myelopathies
HIV, HTLV-1, VDRL serologies: rule out infectious myelopathies
Paraneoplastic antibody panel: if atypical presentation or high suspicion
Complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, ESR, CRP for baseline

imaging.details: May show periaqueductal, periventricular, and medullary lesions; area postrema involvement (dorsal medulla) highly characteristic; diencephalic lesions around third ventricle; longitudinal extensive brainstem lesions; PRES-like pattern
imaging.typicalFindings: AQP4-rich regions: periaqueductal gray, area postrema, circumventricular organs; MS-typical periventular pattern absent

imaging.modality: MRI cervical and thoracic spine with contrast
imaging.details: Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM) >3 vertebral segments; central gray matter predilection; cord swelling and enhancement acutely; may show T2-hyperintensity centrally
imaging.typicalFindings: Long segment central cord T2 hyperintensity, patchy or ring enhancement, cord expansion during acute phase

imaging.modality: MRI orbits with fat suppression and contrast
imaging.details: Optic nerve enhancement, bilateral or unilateral; often affects posterior optic nerve and chiasm; extensive involvement >50% of nerve length
imaging.typicalFindings: Long segment optic nerve T2 hyperintensity and enhancement, chiasmal involvement more common than MS

imaging.details: Retinal nerve fiber layer thinning after optic neuritis; ganglion cell layer thinning; more severe than MS-associated optic neuritis
imaging.typicalFindings: Significant RNFL thinning in affected eyes; may detect subclinical involvement

imaging.details: P100 latency prolonged or absent in acute optic neuritis; amplitude reduction more prominent than latency delay compared to MS
imaging.typicalFindings: Prolonged or absent P100 waveform; greater amplitude reduction than MS

management.protocolReference: International Consensus Diagnostic Criteria for NMOSD (Wingerchuk et al., Neurology 2015) and AAN Practice Guideline Updates
acuteTreatment: High-dose IV methylprednisolone: 1000 mg daily for 5 days, first-line for acute attacks
Plasma exchange (PLEX): for steroid-refractory attacks, 5-7 exchanges over 10-14 days; initiate early if poor response to steroids
IV cyclophosphamide: reserved for life-threatening attacks unresponsive to steroids and PLEX
Oral steroid taper over 4-6 weeks after IV steroids to prevent early relapse

preventiveImmunosuppression.monitoring: CBC and LFTs monthly initially, then every 3 months
preventiveImmunosuppression.details: First-line maintenance therapy; most effective for relapse prevention; onset 4-8 weeks
preventiveImmunosuppression.adverseEffects: GI upset, bone marrow suppression, infections, teratogenicity

preventiveImmunosuppression.dose: 1000 mg IV repeated after 2 weeks, then 1000 mg every 6 months or CD19-guided dosing
preventiveImmunosuppression.monitoring: CD19/CD20 counts before each infusion to guide retreatment; quantitative immunoglobulins, CBC
preventiveImmunosuppression.details: First-line or second-line; B-cell depletion reduces relapses significantly; onset 4-6 weeks
preventiveImmunosuppression.adverseEffects: Infusion reactions, PML risk (very low), hypogammaglobulinemia, infections

preventiveImmunosuppression.dose: 2-3 mg/kg/day, often with prednisone bridge for first 4-6 months
preventiveImmunosuppression.monitoring: CBC and LFTs every 2 weeks for first 2 months, then every 3 months; TPMT genotyping before initiation
preventiveImmunosuppression.details: First-line maintenance in many countries; onset 3-6 months
preventiveImmunosuppression.adverseEffects: Hepatotoxicity, bone marrow suppression, GI intolerance, malignancy risk

preventiveImmunosuppression.dose: 900 mg IV weekly for 4 doses, then 1200 mg every 2 weeks
preventiveImmunosuppression.monitoring: Meningococcal vaccination required before initiation; monitor for infections
preventiveImmunosuppression.details: FDA-approved for AQP4-IgG positive NMOSD; complement inhibitor, highly effective
preventiveImmunosuppression.adverseEffects: Meningococcal infection risk, upper respiratory infections, headache

preventiveImmunosuppression.dose: 300 mg IV on day 1 and 15, then 300 mg every 6 months
preventiveImmunosuppression.details: FDA-approved for AQP4-IgG positive NMOSD; B-cell depleting agent

preventiveImmunosuppression.dose: 120 mg SC every 2 weeks for 3 doses, then every 4 weeks
preventiveImmunosuppression.details: FDA-approved for AQP4-IgG positive NMOSD; IL-6 receptor inhibition
preventiveImmunosuppression.adverseEffects: Infections, injection site reactions, neutropenia, elevated LFTs

symptomaticManagement: Neuropathic pain: gabapentin, pregabalin, amitriptyline, duloxetine
Spasticity: baclofen, tizanidine, gabapentin, botulinum toxin injections
Bladder dysfunction: anticholinergics for detrusor overactivity; intermittent catheterization for retention
Bowel dysfunction: bowel regimen, fiber supplementation, scheduled toileting
Fatigue: modafinil, amantadine, energy conservation strategies
Mood disorders: SSRIs, counseling, cognitive behavioral therapy
Osteoporosis prevention: calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonates if on chronic steroids
pathways: Acute attack: IV methylprednisolone x5 days; if inadequate response within 5-7 days, initiate PLEX
Maintenance: select first-line agent based on patient factors (mycophenolate, rituximab, azathioprine); AQP4-positive patients may benefit from eculizumab/satralizumab/inebilizumab
Monitoring: clinical assessments every 3-6 months; MRI brain/spine annually or with relapses; OCT annually for optic nerve monitoring; serologic monitoring for medication safety

pathophysiology.primaryMechanism: Autoantibody-mediated attack against aquaporin-4 (AQP4) water channels on astrocyte foot processes, causing astrocytopathy with secondary oligodendrocyte injury and demyelination
humoralImmunity: AQP4-IgG predominantly IgG1 subclass, activates classical complement pathway
Complement-dependent cytotoxicity leads to astrocyte death
Antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity via NK cells and macrophages
astrocyteInjury: AQP4 loss from astrocyte endfeet at blood-brain barrier
Complement deposition (C5b-9 membrane attack complex) on astrocytes
Astrocyte necrosis and detachment from glia limitans
Release of inflammatory mediators recruiting granulocytes and macrophages
pathophysiology.demyelination: Secondary to astrocyte loss; oligodendrocytes are not the primary target but are injured by the inflammatory milieu; myelin loss is secondary to the astrocytopathic process
pathophysiology.MOGIgGPathophysiology: MOG-IgG targets myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein on outermost myelin lamellae; predominantly complement-mediated demyelination with preserved astrocytes; distinct from AQP4-NMOSD pathogenesis
tissuePathology: Perivascular inflammatory infiltrates with eosinophils and neutrophils (distinct from MS)
Loss of AQP4 immunoreactivity in active lesions
Complement deposition in a characteristic rim pattern
Necrosis and cavitation in severe lesions
Relative preservation of myelin in early lesions compared to MS
triggerFactors: Molecular mimicry: pathogens containing AQP4-like epitopes
Loss of immune tolerance to AQP4 in genetically susceptible individuals
Postpartum period: immune modulation changes increase relapse risk

complications: Permanent visual impairment: bilateral optic nerve damage leading to legal blindness in 30-50% untreated
Paraplegia or quadriplegia from recurrent or severe transverse myelitis
Respiratory failure due to cervical myelitis affecting phrenic nerve motor neurons (C3-C5)
Neurogenic bladder: urinary retention, recurrent urinary tract infections, renal impairment
Chronic neuropathic pain: often severe and refractory to treatment
Pressure ulcers and contractures from immobility
Secondary infections from immunosuppression including PML with rituximab and mycophenolate
Postpartum relapses: 40% risk within 6 months after delivery if untreated
Catastrophic brain syndrome: large tumefactive brain lesions, encephalopathy, seizures, cerebral edema
mustNotMiss: Area postrema syndrome (intractable hiccups, nausea, vomiting) is highly specific for NMOSD and often misdiagnosed as gastroenteritis
Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>3 vertebral segments) on spine MRI is the classic imaging hallmark
AQP4-IgG testing must be done with cell-based assay for optimal sensitivity and specificity
Differentiate from MS early because MS disease-modifying therapies (interferon-beta, fingolimod, natalizumab) can worsen NMOSD
Check AQP4-IgG in all patients with bilateral optic neuritis or recurrent optic neuritis
Cervical myelitis may cause respiratory compromise - monitor FVC closely
CSF oligoclonal bands are often negative in NMOSD (vs positive in 85-90% of MS)
Do not start interferon-beta or TNF-alpha inhibitors if NMOSD is suspected - they can exacerbate disease
emergencyPriority: HIGH - Acute attacks require urgent immunotherapy to prevent permanent disability
diagnosticClues: Bilateral optic neuritis or recurrent unilateral optic neuritis
Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis on spine MRI (>3 segments)
Area postrema syndrome: intractable hiccups, nausea, vomiting as presenting symptom
Serum AQP4-IgG positive by cell-based assay
MRI brain: periaqueductal, area postrema, and diencephalic lesions
CSF neutrophil-predominant pleocytosis (>50 cells) during acute attack
Simultaneous optic neuritis and transverse myelitis
Presence of other autoimmune disease (SLE, Sjogren, MG, thyroiditis)
Female patient with severe relapsing opticospinal disease
exclusionClues: Short-segment spinal cord lesions (<2 vertebral segments) more suggestive of MS
Multiple periventricular and juxtacortical white matter plaques typical for MS
CSF oligoclonal bands present strongly suggests MS rather than NMOSD
AQP4-IgG negative by high-quality cell-based assay significantly reduces likelihood
MOG-IgG positive suggests MOGAD rather than AQP4-NMOSD (different prognosis and treatment approach)
Barkhof or McDonald criteria for MS met on brain MRI favors MS diagnosis
Mild, steroid-responsive optic neuritis with full recovery typical for MS
Absence of LETM, area postrema syndrome, or bilateral optic neuritis
